problem_statement: Macros for Romance-language and Dutch customers are outdated copies that haven't been refreshed in over a year, and at least one English/German macro still references a UI element that no longer exists, causing customer confusion.
ai_solution_concept: An LLM can compare macro content against current help-centre articles or UI change logs to flag stale instructions for human review, enabling proactive macro hygiene.

sub_scores.name: volume
sub_scores.rationale: Romance-language and Dutch tickets represent the 'rest' beyond the 60% German and 30% English, so roughly 10% of ~2,100 weekly tickets (~210/week). This is a meaningful but not dominant slice of volume, fitting the 'material' anchor of 10–25% (on the lower end).
sub_scores.evidence_quote: About 2,100 tickets a week into Tier 1. Sixty percent German, thirty English, the rest split across French, Spanish, Italian, Dutch.

sub_scores.name: hours_saved
sub_scores.rationale: The stale macro problem affects a subset of the ~45% recurring-pattern tickets, but only for the Romance-language/Dutch queue which is a small fraction of total volume. The transcript does not quantify agent-hours lost to stale macros specifically, suggesting a minor rather than material time cost.
sub_scores.evidence_quote: Roughly 45% of Tier 1 tickets are recurring patterns. Resetting employee passwords, re-sending mobile app invites... We have macros for most of them, in English and German. The Romance-language and Dutch macros are usually translated copies that haven't been updated in a year.

sub_scores.name: customer_friction
sub_scores.rationale: The transcript explicitly describes customer confusion caused by stale macros referencing UI elements that no longer exist, and a 'steady drip of escalations' tied to mismatched instructions — indicating a recurring, noticeable source of customer pain beyond mere annoyance.
sub_scores.evidence_quote: The Personio UI changed in November and we still have macros telling customers to click "Mitarbeiterstammdaten" in a menu that no longer exists.

sub_scores.name: data_availability
sub_scores.rationale: Help-centre articles exist and macros are in Zendesk, but the transcript notes articles are 'uneven in non-English languages' and there is no mention of structured UI change logs being readily available, requiring some effort to assemble the comparison inputs.
sub_scores.evidence_quote: Fin's answers are only as good as the help-centre articles, and those are uneven in non-English languages.

sub_scores.name: llm_capability
sub_scores.rationale: Comparing macro text against help-centre articles or UI change logs to flag discrepancies is a textbook LLM strength — semantic comparison, extraction, and classification — well within current model capability with a careful prompt.
sub_scores.evidence_quote: We still have macros telling customers to click "Mitarbeiterstammdaten" in a menu that no longer exists.

sub_scores.name: process_stability
sub_scores.rationale: The macro drift problem is described as ongoing and chronic rather than a one-off, but the underlying UI and help-centre content changes frequently (e.g. November UI change still unaddressed), meaning the process the AI integrates into is somewhat unstable and would require periodic re-runs rather than a fully static pipeline.
sub_scores.evidence_quote: Macros drift constantly. The Personio UI changed in November and we still have macros telling customers to click "Mitarbeiterstammdaten" in a menu that no longer exists.

sub_scores.name: dev_effort
sub_scores.rationale: The solution is primarily a comparison and flagging task — fetch macro content, fetch help-centre articles, run an LLM diff, surface flags for human review. This is largely integration and prompt work, estimable at one to two weeks of engineering effort.
sub_scores.evidence_quote: An LLM can compare macro content against current help-centre articles or UI change logs to flag stale instructions for human review, enabling proactive macro hygiene.

sub_scores.name: api_cost
sub_scores.rationale: Macro hygiene is a batch, low-frequency task (run periodically, not on every ticket). The corpus of macros is small relative to ticket volume, so LLM API spend would be negligible — well under $200/month.
sub_scores.evidence_quote: The Romance-language and Dutch macros are usually translated copies that haven't been updated in a year.

sub_scores.name: maintenance_burden
sub_scores.rationale: Once the prompt is tuned to compare macro instructions against article content, maintenance is primarily re-running the job after UI or product changes. No complex retraining or eval pipeline is needed; occasional prompt tweaks as the product evolves would suffice.
sub_scores.evidence_quote: Macros drift constantly. The Personio UI changed in November and we still have macros telling customers to click "Mitarbeiterstammdaten" in a menu that no longer exists.
